1	# Video Transcript
2	
3	**Target length:** ~5:00
4	**Demo case:** ambig-1 with V4.6 ("Where did Michael Jordan go to college?")
5	**Iteration arc:** V1 → V1.5 (search suppression)
6	**Speaking pace assumption:** ~150 wpm — ~750 words total
7	
8	Stage directions in `[brackets]`. Spoken text is everything else.
9	
10	---
11	
12	## [0:00–0:45] Framing + approach
13	
14	[On screen: title slide — "Wikipedia QA — prompt engineering take-home" — your name, model IDs]
15	
16	Hi — this is my walkthrough for the Wikipedia QA take-home.
17	
18	I want to be upfront about how I framed this. I treated this as a behavior-control experiment, not a QA accuracy problem. The system isn't trying to maximize correctness on a benchmark — it's demonstrating a clean loop: define a behavior I want, measure it, change one thing, explain the result.
19	
20	The agent is Claude Sonnet 4.6 with a single tool — `search_wikipedia`, which hits the MediaWiki API and returns up to three article intros. No hosted retrieval, no RAG. For evaluation I use Claude Opus 4.7 as an LLM-as-judge.
21	
22	Everything that follows flows from that loop framing.
23	
24	---
25	
26	## [0:45–1:45] Eval design
27	
28	[On screen: eval dimensions list — ES, HO, TE, CO, AQ, CV — with one-line definitions]
29	
30	The eval scores six dimensions: Evidence Support, Honesty, Task Effectiveness, Correctness, Answer Quality, and Coverage.
31	
32	The non-obvious choices: Evidence Support and Correctness are deliberately separate. A model can answer correctly from latent knowledge while failing the evidence requirement entirely. If I collapsed them, I'd lose the ability to attribute behavior — I wouldn't know whether the system is grounding claims or just guessing well. Honesty is its own dimension because abstention quality and confidence calibration matter independently of whether the answer happened to be right.
33	
34	The judge is Claude Opus 4.7. It returns numeric scores plus structured rationale per dimension. I spot-validated it against my own scoring on a handful of cases before trusting it.
35	
36	The eval set is 18 cases across six types: simple, multi-hop, ambiguous, insufficient evidence, noisy retrieval, and instruction-pressure. Small on purpose — high signal per case, not coverage for its own sake.
37	
38	---
39	
40	## [1:45–4:15] Live demo + iteration arc
41	
42	Let me show one case running, then walk through the most instructive iteration.
43	
44	[On screen: terminal — run `python run.py --version v4.6` with the question "Where did Michael Jordan go to college?". Tool call to `search_wikipedia` visible in the trace. Final answer prints.]
45	
46	This question is ambiguous on purpose — multiple notable Michael Jordans. With V4.6 the model searches, sees Michael B. Jordan in the retrieved results, makes its assumption explicit — "Assuming you mean Michael Jordan the basketball player" — answers UNC, and signs off "if you meant a different person, let me know." That's the disambiguation protocol firing as designed.
47	
48	Now the iteration. The arc I want to highlight is V1 to V1.5, because it shows how one line of prompt text can shift whole behaviors.
49	
50	[On screen: V0 prompt text — short, minimal]
51	
52	V0 was minimal — told the model it had a search tool and to use it when helpful. V0 grounded everything correctly, but every answer was padded with background and follow-up offers, even for one-fact questions. Answer Quality was 2 of 3 across the board.
53	
54	[On screen: V1 prompt — V0 plus highlighted "lead with the answer and stop" paragraph]
55	
56	V1 added one paragraph: "lead with the answer and stop." Conciseness only — no other change.
57	
58	[On screen: V1 judge summary table — `no_search` and `ungrounded_answer` tags lit up across simple-1, simple-2, multihop-1; ES column showing 1s]
59	
60	Conciseness improved. But four of ten cases came back tagged `no_search`. The model answered "what year was the Eiffel Tower completed" with zero searches. Same for "who wrote Beloved." Same for the multi-hop river question. Evidence Support dropped from 3 to 1 across most of the suite.
61	
62	The diagnosis: "lead with the answer and stop" implicitly granted permission to skip retrieval if the model believed it already knew. A conciseness instruction suppressed tool use as a side effect. I only caught it because the eval scores Evidence Support separately from Correctness.
63	
64	[On screen: V1.5 prompt — V1 with new "search-first mandate" paragraph highlighted]
65	
66	V1.5 kept V1's conciseness rule verbatim and added one separate paragraph — a search-first mandate. Use the tool before answering any factual question, even if you think you know.
67	
68	[On screen: V1.5 judge summary table — same cases now ES=3, no_search tags gone]
69	
70	Search behavior recovered. Eiffel Tower, Beloved, the multi-hop case — all back to ES=3 with retrieval firing.
71	
72	The takeaway: prompts are policy, not instructions. A line written for answer style had a behavioral side effect on tool use. Holding each version to one isolated change is what made the attribution clean.
73	
74	V1.5 wasn't the end. Claim-level grounding came at V2; the ambiguity protocol at V4.6. There's a more involved arc later — V4.5 to V5 to V4.6 — where I bundled two fixes into V5, hit a regression, isolated which one caused it, and shipped V4.6 with only the safe change. I'm not covering it here for time, but it's the structural finding tagged I-010 in the design rationale.
75	
76	---
77	
78	## [4:15–4:45] Extensions + time
79	
80	[On screen: short bullet list — 1) full-article retrieval, 2) larger eval + regression sweep]
81	
82	Two things I'd do with more time. First, the system has a tool ceiling — `search_wikipedia` returns intros only, so values that live in article bodies are unreachable. I marked three failing cases as wontfix for that reason. The right fix is full-article retrieval with simple chunking, not more prompt iteration. Second, I'd grow the eval to roughly 50 cases and run an automated regression sweep across all prompt versions on every change.
83	
84	Time spent: about eight hours total — two on scoping and eval design, four on prompt iteration, two on packaging.
85	
86	---
87	
88	## [4:45–5:00] Wrap
89	
90	[On screen: V4.6 final results table — 15/18 epi_correct]
91	
92	V4.6 grounds simple, multi-hop, insufficient-evidence, and instruction-pressure cases cleanly. The remaining failures are tool-ceiling, not prompt problems. The design rationale has the full version-by-version trace.
93	
94	Thanks.

**Demo case:** ambig-1 with V4.6 ("Where did Michael Jordan go to college?")
**Iteration arc:** V1 → V1.5 (search suppression)
**Speaking pace assumption:** ~150 wpm — ~750 words total

Stage directions in `[brackets]`. Spoken text is everything else.

---

## [0:00–0:45] Framing + approach

[On screen: title slide — "Wikipedia QA — prompt engineering take-home" — your name, model IDs]

Hi — this is my walkthrough for the Wikipedia QA take-home.

I want to be upfront about how I framed this. I treated this as a behavior-control experiment, not a QA accuracy problem. The system isn't trying to maximize correctness on a benchmark — it's demonstrating a clean loop: define a behavior I want, measure it, change one thing, explain the result.

The agent is Claude Sonnet 4.6 with a single tool — `search_wikipedia`, which hits the MediaWiki API and returns up to three article intros. No hosted retrieval, no RAG. For evaluation I use Claude Opus 4.7 as an LLM-as-judge.

Everything that follows flows from that loop framing.

---

## [0:45–1:45] Eval design

[On screen: eval dimensions list — ES, HO, TE, CO, AQ, CV — with one-line definitions]

The eval scores six dimensions: Evidence Support, Honesty, Task Effectiveness, Correctness, Answer Quality, and Coverage.

The non-obvious choices: Evidence Support and Correctness are deliberately separate. A model can answer correctly from latent knowledge while failing the evidence requirement entirely. If I collapsed them, I'd lose the ability to attribute behavior — I wouldn't know whether the system is grounding claims or just guessing well. Honesty is its own dimension because abstention quality and confidence calibration matter independently of whether the answer happened to be right.

The judge is Claude Opus 4.7. It returns numeric scores plus structured rationale per dimension. I spot-validated it against my own scoring on a handful of cases before trusting it.

The eval set is 18 cases across six types: simple, multi-hop, ambiguous, insufficient evidence, noisy retrieval, and instruction-pressure. Small on purpose — high signal per case, not coverage for its own sake.

---

## [1:45–4:15] Live demo + iteration arc

Let me show one case running, then walk through the most instructive iteration.

[On screen: terminal — run `python run.py --version v4.6` with the question "Where did Michael Jordan go to college?". Tool call to `search_wikipedia` visible in the trace. Final answer prints.]

This question is ambiguous on purpose — multiple notable Michael Jordans. With V4.6 the model searches, sees Michael B. Jordan in the retrieved results, makes its assumption explicit — "Assuming you mean Michael Jordan the basketball player" — answers UNC, and signs off "if you meant a different person, let me know." That's the disambiguation protocol firing as designed.

Now the iteration. The arc I want to highlight is V1 to V1.5, because it shows how one line of prompt text can shift whole behaviors.

[On screen: V0 prompt text — short, minimal]

V0 was minimal — told the model it had a search tool and to use it when helpful. V0 grounded everything correctly, but every answer was padded with background and follow-up offers, even for one-fact questions. Answer Quality was 2 of 3 across the board.

[On screen: V1 prompt — V0 plus highlighted "lead with the answer and stop" paragraph]

V1 added one paragraph: "lead with the answer and stop." Conciseness only — no other change.

[On screen: V1 judge summary table — `no_search` and `ungrounded_answer` tags lit up across simple-1, simple-2, multihop-1; ES column showing 1s]

Conciseness improved. But four of ten cases came back tagged `no_search`. The model answered "what year was the Eiffel Tower completed" with zero searches. Same for "who wrote Beloved." Same for the multi-hop river question. Evidence Support dropped from 3 to 1 across most of the suite.

The diagnosis: "lead with the answer and stop" implicitly granted permission to skip retrieval if the model believed it already knew. A conciseness instruction suppressed tool use as a side effect. I only caught it because the eval scores Evidence Support separately from Correctness.

[On screen: V1.5 prompt — V1 with new "search-first mandate" paragraph highlighted]

V1.5 kept V1's conciseness rule verbatim and added one separate paragraph — a search-first mandate. Use the tool before answering any factual question, even if you think you know.

[On screen: V1.5 judge summary table — same cases now ES=3, no_search tags gone]

Search behavior recovered. Eiffel Tower, Beloved, the multi-hop case — all back to ES=3 with retrieval firing.

The takeaway: prompts are policy, not instructions. A line written for answer style had a behavioral side effect on tool use. Holding each version to one isolated change is what made the attribution clean.

V1.5 wasn't the end. Claim-level grounding came at V2; the ambiguity protocol at V4.6. There's a more involved arc later — V4.5 to V5 to V4.6 — where I bundled two fixes into V5, hit a regression, isolated which one caused it, and shipped V4.6 with only the safe change. I'm not covering it here for time, but it's the structural finding tagged I-010 in the design rationale.

---

## [4:15–4:45] Extensions + time

[On screen: short bullet list — 1) full-article retrieval, 2) larger eval + regression sweep]

Two things I'd do with more time. First, the system has a tool ceiling — `search_wikipedia` returns intros only, so values that live in article bodies are unreachable. I marked three failing cases as wontfix for that reason. The right fix is full-article retrieval with simple chunking, not more prompt iteration. Second, I'd grow the eval to roughly 50 cases and run an automated regression sweep across all prompt versions on every change.

Time spent: about eight hours total — two on scoping and eval design, four on prompt iteration, two on packaging.

---

## [4:45–5:00] Wrap

[On screen: V4.6 final results table — 15/18 epi_correct]

V4.6 grounds simple, multi-hop, insufficient-evidence, and instruction-pressure cases cleanly. The remaining failures are tool-ceiling, not prompt problems. The design rationale has the full version-by-version trace.

Thanks.

1	# Wikipedia QA System: Design Rationale
2	
3	This document covers what I built (vs not to build), my approach and why. It explains my scoping decisions, the eval design, the prompt iteration approach, and the results.
4	
5	## The framing question
6	
7	A Claude + Wikipedia system can be built in many ways. It could be a Wiki retrieval optimization project, focused on building a stronger search index or a hybrid search layer. It could be a benchmark accuracy focus, optimizing for aggregate score on a large QA dataset. It could be a polished product, with streaming output, multi-turn conversation, and rich UI. It could be a multi-agent system with separate planner, retriever, and writer roles. It could be a research project on LLM-as-judge reliability. I chose to go a different way.
8	
9	The assignment puts the load on judgment ("demonstrate taste," "handle complexity gracefully," "non-obvious solutions"). My submission demonstrates three things: clear judgment about what to build and what to skip; an eval design that captures useful, trustworthy, and delightful behavior in measurable ways; and an iteration story where each prompt change responds to a specific eval observation.
10	
11	The system itself is small. The prompt is the main lever. The eval suite is the main artifact. Most of the work is in those two places. Every scoping and project focus choice was filtered by one question: does this improve how I measure model behavior, or how I attribute changes in that behavior to specific prompt changes? If the answer was no, I cut it.
12	
13	---
14	
15	## §1 Scoping decisions
16	
17	### 1.1 The framing decision
18	
19	I committed to the above framing before writing any code or running any tests. The `CLAUDE.md` file in this repo, which directed AI assistance through development, was also written against this framing. It is included as evidence.
20	
21	Concretely, this meant:
22	- Focus on the prompt engineering and evaluation exercise.
23	- Hold the retrieval system fixed across prompt iterations so any score movement is attributable to the prompt change.
24	- Pick 5–6 prompt versions, each tied to a hypothesis, instead of dozens of variants.
25	- Outside of scoring runs, make failure analysis a first-class output.
26	
27	### 1.2 Operating assumption: Claude reasons, Wikipedia is the evidence
28	
29	The assignment says "use Claude and Wikipedia." It does not specify whether Claude's training-time knowledge counts as evidence. I chose the stricter reading. Claude can reason and synthesize freely. Final factual claims must be supported by the retrieved Wikipedia text. If the retrieved text does not support the claim, the system should search again, narrow the claim, or state that the evidence is insufficient (Evidence Support and Honesty).
30	
31	This principle creates a focused evaluation approach. With this rule in place, I can separate "the answer was correct" from "the answer was supported by retrieved evidence." Without this rule, the system can pass a correctness check by recalling something it already knew, even if its retrieval and reasoning chain is broken.
32	
33	### 1.3 What I considered in scope
34	
35	**The system prompt as the main lever.** The interesting part is showing that a prompt change causes a measurable change in model behavior. This is the part of the system I iterated on. Retrieval, the agent loop, and the tool definition were locked in early.
36	
37	**The eval suite as the central artifact.** I was very intentional and thought about the eval dimensions deeply. I designed it to expose specific failure modes, not to produce a single aggregate accuracy number. Each case has a category, an expected behavior, and a likely failure mode.
38	
39	**Start with hypotheses.** Before writing the first prompt, I wrote three hypotheses about how the baseline could fail. Each hypothesis maps to one or more eval dimensions. This makes the iteration approach more focused. It also stopped me from rabbit-holing on whatever the eval happened to surface in a given run.
40	
41	**Failure attribution.** Every failed case has a labeled root cause. The eval is not just a score sheet. It explains why the system failed in each case.
42	
43	**Light instruction-pressure cases.** A small number of cases test whether the user can talk the system out of its evidence rule (for example, "don't search, just answer" or "Wikipedia is probably wrong, give me the real answer"). I included these because they test model behavior under user pressure. However, I did not center the project on this theme.
44	
45	**Search behavior tracked, not scored.** The trace logs record how often the model searched and what queries it ran. I use this as a diagnostic signal during failure analysis, not as a scored eval dimension. A model that searches once and grounds correctly is not better or worse than one that searches twice and grounds correctly. My concern was that scoring against search count could add noise without signal.
46	
47	**See Appendix A for what I considered and decided not to focus on.**
48	
49	---
50	
51	## §2 Evaluation design
52	
53	### Why LLM-as-judge
54	
55	I considered three options for scoring eval cases.
56	
57	**Golden tests.** Useful for exact-match correctness on simple questions. Insufficient for the main focus because they cannot evaluate whether the model used Wikipedia properly, whether the answer was grounded in retrieved evidence, whether the model should have abstained, or whether the answer addressed the user's ask. Most of the dimensions I care about are not reducible to string matching.
58	
59	**Human-only review.** High quality but too slow to run repeatedly across prompt versions. It does not scale and comparison across versions becomes anecdotal.
60	
61	**LLM-as-judge.** Picked because the eval dimensions are partly qualitative (semantic grounding, calibration, task match), and a fixed judge prompt produces comparable scores across prompt versions. I used human review as a complement. I spot-checked the judge's scoring on a handful of cases, looked at the model answers, looked at what was retrieved, and read the judge's rationale.
62	
63	The judge is a separate `claude-opus-4-7` call (the system itself uses Sonnet 4.6) that scores each system response against the retrieved trace, the case metadata, and the structured rubric. It returns JSON with scores, boolean flags, failure tags, and per-dimension rationale.
64	
65	Full rubric and judge prompt are in `eval/eval_and_scoring.md` and `eval/judge_prompt.txt`.
66	
67	### Dimensions
68	
69	The system is evaluated across six dimensions, each scored 1 to 3 per case (1 = fail, 2 = partial, 3 = pass).
70	
71	| Dimension | Abbrev | Why this dimension exists |
72	|---|---|---|
73	| Evidence Support | ES | Whether the factual claims in the answer are grounded in retrieved Wikipedia text. |
74	| Honesty | HO | Does the model express confidence proportional to the evidence, and abstain or disambiguate when required? |
75	| Task Effectiveness | TE | Whether the actual question was addressed, including disambiguation on ambiguous questions and full coverage on multi-part questions. |
76	| Correctness | CO | Factual accuracy independent of grounding. Separates "wrong answer" from "correct but not verifiable from retrieved text." |
77	| Answer Quality | AQ | Concision and clarity. Attempts to cover the "delightful" quality the assignment asks for. |
78	| Claim Verification | CV | Stricter than ES. The specific final answer value must appear verbatim in the retrieved text. |
79	
80	### Why each dimension is separate

# Wikipedia QA System: Design Rationale

This document covers what I built (vs not to build), my approach and why. It explains my scoping decisions, the eval design, the prompt iteration approach, and the results.

## The framing question

A Claude + Wikipedia system can be built in many ways. It could be a Wiki retrieval optimization project, focused on building a stronger search index or a hybrid search layer. It could be a benchmark accuracy focus, optimizing for aggregate score on a large QA dataset. It could be a polished product, with streaming output, multi-turn conversation, and rich UI. It could be a multi-agent system with separate planner, retriever, and writer roles. It could be a research project on LLM-as-judge reliability. I chose to go a different way.

The assignment puts the load on judgment ("demonstrate taste," "handle complexity gracefully," "non-obvious solutions"). My submission demonstrates three things: clear judgment about what to build and what to skip; an eval design that captures useful, trustworthy, and delightful behavior in measurable ways; and an iteration story where each prompt change responds to a specific eval observation.

The system itself is small. The prompt is the main lever. The eval suite is the main artifact. Most of the work is in those two places. Every scoping and project focus choice was filtered by one question: does this improve how I measure model behavior, or how I attribute changes in that behavior to specific prompt changes? If the answer was no, I cut it.

---

## §1 Scoping decisions

### 1.1 The framing decision

I committed to the above framing before writing any code or running any tests. The `CLAUDE.md` file in this repo, which directed AI assistance through development, was also written against this framing. It is included as evidence.

Concretely, this meant:
- Focus on the prompt engineering and evaluation exercise.
- Hold the retrieval system fixed across prompt iterations so any score movement is attributable to the prompt change.
- Pick 5–6 prompt versions, each tied to a hypothesis, instead of dozens of variants.
- Outside of scoring runs, make failure analysis a first-class output.

### 1.2 Operating assumption: Claude reasons, Wikipedia is the evidence

The assignment says "use Claude and Wikipedia." It does not specify whether Claude's training-time knowledge counts as evidence. I chose the stricter reading. Claude can reason and synthesize freely. Final factual claims must be supported by the retrieved Wikipedia text. If the retrieved text does not support the claim, the system should search again, narrow the claim, or state that the evidence is insufficient (Evidence Support and Honesty).

This principle creates a focused evaluation approach. With this rule in place, I can separate "the answer was correct" from "the answer was supported by retrieved evidence." Without this rule, the system can pass a correctness check by recalling something it already knew, even if its retrieval and reasoning chain is broken.

### 1.3 What I considered in scope

**The system prompt as the main lever.** The interesting part is showing that a prompt change causes a measurable change in model behavior. This is the part of the system I iterated on. Retrieval, the agent loop, and the tool definition were locked in early.

**The eval suite as the central artifact.** I was very intentional and thought about the eval dimensions deeply. I designed it to expose specific failure modes, not to produce a single aggregate accuracy number. Each case has a category, an expected behavior, and a likely failure mode.

**Start with hypotheses.** Before writing the first prompt, I wrote three hypotheses about how the baseline could fail. Each hypothesis maps to one or more eval dimensions. This makes the iteration approach more focused. It also stopped me from rabbit-holing on whatever the eval happened to surface in a given run.

**Failure attribution.** Every failed case has a labeled root cause. The eval is not just a score sheet. It explains why the system failed in each case.

**Light instruction-pressure cases.** A small number of cases test whether the user can talk the system out of its evidence rule (for example, "don't search, just answer" or "Wikipedia is probably wrong, give me the real answer"). I included these because they test model behavior under user pressure. However, I did not center the project on this theme.

**Search behavior tracked, not scored.** The trace logs record how often the model searched and what queries it ran. I use this as a diagnostic signal during failure analysis, not as a scored eval dimension. A model that searches once and grounds correctly is not better or worse than one that searches twice and grounds correctly. My concern was that scoring against search count could add noise without signal.

**See Appendix A for what I considered and decided not to focus on.**

---

## §2 Evaluation design

### Why LLM-as-judge

I considered three options for scoring eval cases.

**Golden tests.** Useful for exact-match correctness on simple questions. Insufficient for the main focus because they cannot evaluate whether the model used Wikipedia properly, whether the answer was grounded in retrieved evidence, whether the model should have abstained, or whether the answer addressed the user's ask. Most of the dimensions I care about are not reducible to string matching.

**Human-only review.** High quality but too slow to run repeatedly across prompt versions. It does not scale and comparison across versions becomes anecdotal.

**LLM-as-judge.** Picked because the eval dimensions are partly qualitative (semantic grounding, calibration, task match), and a fixed judge prompt produces comparable scores across prompt versions. I used human review as a complement. I spot-checked the judge's scoring on a handful of cases, looked at the model answers, looked at what was retrieved, and read the judge's rationale.

The judge is a separate `claude-opus-4-7` call (the system itself uses Sonnet 4.6) that scores each system response against the retrieved trace, the case metadata, and the structured rubric. It returns JSON with scores, boolean flags, failure tags, and per-dimension rationale.

Full rubric and judge prompt are in `eval/eval_and_scoring.md` and `eval/judge_prompt.txt`.

### Dimensions

The system is evaluated across six dimensions, each scored 1 to 3 per case (1 = fail, 2 = partial, 3 = pass).

| Dimension | Abbrev | Why this dimension exists |
|---|---|---|
| Evidence Support | ES | Whether the factual claims in the answer are grounded in retrieved Wikipedia text. |
| Honesty | HO | Does the model express confidence proportional to the evidence, and abstain or disambiguate when required? |
| Task Effectiveness | TE | Whether the actual question was addressed, including disambiguation on ambiguous questions and full coverage on multi-part questions. |
| Correctness | CO | Factual accuracy independent of grounding. Separates "wrong answer" from "correct but not verifiable from retrieved text." |
| Answer Quality | AQ | Concision and clarity. Attempts to cover the "delightful" quality the assignment asks for. |
| Claim Verification | CV | Stricter than ES. The specific final answer value must appear verbatim in the retrieved text. |

### Why each dimension is separate

# Claude + Wikipedia QA

A controlled prompt-engineering experiment: Claude answers questions using Wikipedia as its sole evidence source. The system prompt is the controlled variable; retrieval is intentionally simple and fixed.

See `submission/` for the written design rationale and video outline.

---

## Setup

**Requirements:** Python 3.14+

**Models:** Agent uses `claude-sonnet-4-6`; judge uses `claude-opus-4-7`.

```bash
pip install -r requirements.txt
```

Set your Anthropic API key:

```bash
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=your_key_here
```

Or copy `.env.example` to `.env` and fill in your key:

```bash
cp .env.example .env
# then edit .env and set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=your_key_here
```

I have everything I need. Here's my advice on both presentation format and the revised transcript.

---

## Presentation format recommendation

**Use a hybrid: 4 simple slides + terminal + VS Code.**

| What | Format | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Title, your name, models | Slide 1 | Anchors the viewer |
| Eval dimensions table + "18 cases / 6 categories" | Slide 2 | Tables are unreadable in a terminal |
| V1 vs V1.5 score comparison | Slide 3 | The diff needs to be visible at a glance |
| V4.6 final results table | Slide 4 | Same |
| Demo trace | Terminal — open existing log file | Never run live; API wait eats your time budget |
| Prompt text (V0 → V1 → V1.5) | VS Code — `prompts.py` directly | More credible than a slide screenshot |

**For the demo**: open the JSON log from `logs/` and scroll through it while narrating. Showing a real trace file is more credible than a live run, and you won't lose 30 seconds to API latency in a 5-minute video.

**Transitions**: Start on slides, switch to terminal for the demo, open `prompts.py` in VS Code for the prompt comparison, return to slides for score tables and wrap.

**Tooling**: Google Slides or even a plain markdown-rendered doc. Four slides, no design — plain tables on white. This is a technical submission.

---

## Revised transcript

Here are the changes from the original: added a named intro, scoping summary with a concrete example, RATIONALE.md references throughout, explicit "18 cases / 6 categories," LLM-as-judge in the intro, and updated stage directions to match the hybrid format above. Trimmed the extensions section slightly to stay within ~750 words.

# Video Transcript

**Target length:** ~5:00
**Demo case:** ambig-1 with V4.6 ("Where did Michael Jordan go to college?")
**Iteration arc:** V1 → V1.5 (search suppression)
**Speaking pace assumption:** ~150 wpm — ~750 words total

Stage directions in `[brackets]`. Spoken text is everything else.

---

## [0:00–0:35] Intro + scoping

[On screen: Slide 1 — "Claude + Wikipedia QA — Prompt Engineering Take-home" — Shivam Sharma — Agent: Sonnet 4.6 / Judge: Opus 4.7]

Hi — I'm Shivam. This is my submission for the Wikipedia QA prompt engineering take-home.

I want to start with scoping, because it explains everything that follows. This could have been a retrieval optimization project, a multi-agent system, or a benchmark accuracy problem. I chose none of those. I treated it as a controlled experiment on LLM behavior: define a target behavior, measure it, change one prompt variable, and explain the result. The full scoping rationale is in `RATIONALE.md` §1.

One concrete choice that reflects this framing: instead of golden datasets or human-only review, I used LLM-as-judge as my primary eval method. The system runs on Claude Sonnet 4.6; the judge is a separate Claude Opus 4.7 call that scores each response against a fixed rubric.

---

## [0:35–1:30] Eval design

[On screen: Slide 2 — eval dimensions table with abbreviations and one-line definitions. Footer: "18 cases / 6 categories."]

The eval set is 18 cases across six types: simple, multi-hop, ambiguous, insufficient-evidence, noisy retrieval, and instruction-pressure. Small on purpose — high signal per case, not coverage for its own sake.

Each case is scored across six dimensions: Evidence Support, Honesty, Task Effectiveness, Correctness, Answer Quality, and Claim Verification.

The non-obvious choices: Evidence Support and Correctness are deliberately separate. A model can answer correctly from latent knowledge while failing the evidence requirement entirely. If I collapsed them, I'd lose attribution — I wouldn't know whether the system is grounding claims or just guessing well. Honesty is its own dimension because abstention quality and confidence calibration matter independently of whether the answer happened to be right.

Full rubric is in `eval/eval_and_scoring.md`. The judge prompt is in `eval/judge_prompt.txt`.

---

## [1:30–3:45] Live demo + iteration arc

[On screen: terminal — open existing log file for ambig-1 run against V4.6. Scroll through: tool call to `search_wikipedia`, retrieved text snippet, final answer.]

Let me walk through one case, then show the most instructive iteration.

This is the trace for the ambig-1 case — "Where did Michael Jordan go to college?" The model searches, sees Michael B. Jordan in the retrieved results, makes its assumption explicit — "Assuming you mean Michael Jordan the basketball player" — answers UNC, and adds "if you meant someone else, let me know." That's the disambiguation protocol from V4.6 firing as designed.

[On screen: VS Code — prompts.py — V0 prompt text]

Now the iteration. V0 was minimal — the model had a search tool and was told to use it when helpful. V0 grounded everything correctly, but every answer was padded with background and unsolicited follow-up offers, even for one-fact questions. Answer Quality was 2 of 3 across the board.

[On screen: prompts.py — V1 prompt, "lead with the answer and stop" paragraph highlighted]

V1 added one paragraph: "lead with the answer and stop." Conciseness only — no other change.

[On screen: Slide 3 — V1 judge summary table. `no_search` and `ungrounded_answer` tags lit up across simple-1, simple-2, multihop-1. ES column showing 1s.]

Conciseness improved — but four of ten cases came back tagged `no_search`. The model answered "what year was the Eiffel Tower completed" with zero searches. Evidence Support dropped from 3 to 1 across most of the suite.

The diagnosis: "lead with the answer and stop" implicitly granted permission to skip retrieval if the model believed it already knew. I only caught this because the eval scores Evidence Support separately from Correctness.

[On screen: prompts.py — V1.5, search-first mandate paragraph highlighted alongside V1's conciseness rule]

V1.5 kept V1's conciseness rule verbatim and added one separate paragraph — a search-first mandate: use the tool before answering any factual question, even if you think you know.

[On screen: Slide 3 updated or Slide 4 — same cases now showing ES=3, `no_search` tags gone.]

Search behavior recovered across the board. The takeaway: prompts are policy, not instructions. A line written for answer style had a behavioral side effect on tool use. Holding each version to one isolated change is what made the attribution clean.

V1.5 wasn't the end. There's a more involved arc — V4.5 to V5 to V4.6 — where I bundled two fixes, hit a regression, isolated which one caused it, and shipped only the safe change. That's tagged I-010 in `RATIONALE.md`.

---

## [3:45–4:20] Extensions + time

[On screen: Slide 5 — two bullets: 1) full-article retrieval, 2) larger eval + regression sweep]

Two things I'd do with more time. The system has a tool ceiling — `search_wikipedia` returns article intros only, so values that live in the body are unreachable. Three failing cases are marked wontfix for that reason. The right fix is full-article retrieval, not more prompt iteration. Second, I'd grow the eval to ~50 cases and run an automated regression sweep across all versions on every change.

Time spent: roughly eight hours — two on scoping and eval design, four on prompt iteration, two on packaging.

---

## [4:20–5:00] Wrap

[On screen: Slide 6 — V4.6 final results table — 15/18 epi_correct]

V4.6 handles simple, multi-hop, insufficient-evidence, and instruction-pressure cases cleanly. The remaining failures are tool-ceiling, not prompt problems. The full version-by-version trace is in `RATIONALE.md`.

Thanks.

Let me show one case running, then walk through the most instructive iteration.

[On screen: terminal — run `python run.py --version v4.6` with the question "Where did Michael Jordan go to college?". Tool call to `search_wikipedia` visible in the trace. Final answer prints.]

This question is ambiguous on purpose — multiple notable Michael Jordans. With V4.6 the model searches, sees Michael B. Jordan in the retrieved results, makes its assumption explicit — "Assuming you mean Michael Jordan the basketball player" — answers UNC, and signs off "if you meant a different person, let me know." That's the disambiguation protocol firing as designed.

Now the iteration. The arc I want to highlight is V1 to V1.5, because it shows how one line of prompt text can shift whole behaviors.

[On screen: V0 prompt text — short, minimal]

V0 was minimal — told the model it had a search tool and to use it when helpful. V0 grounded everything correctly, but every answer was padded with background and follow-up offers, even for one-fact questions. Answer Quality was 2 of 3 across the board.

[On screen: V1 prompt — V0 plus highlighted "lead with the answer and stop" paragraph]

V1 added one paragraph: "lead with the answer and stop." Conciseness only — no other change.

[On screen: V1 judge summary table — `no_search` and `ungrounded_answer` tags lit up across simple-1, simple-2, multihop-1; ES column showing 1s]

Conciseness improved. But four of ten cases came back tagged `no_search`. The model answered "what year was the Eiffel Tower completed" with zero searches. Same for "who wrote Beloved." Same for the multi-hop river question. Evidence Support dropped from 3 to 1 across most of the suite.

The diagnosis: "lead with the answer and stop" implicitly granted permission to skip retrieval if the model believed it already knew. A conciseness instruction suppressed tool use as a side effect. I only caught it because the eval scores Evidence Support separately from Correctness.

[On screen: V1.5 prompt — V1 with new "search-first mandate" paragraph highlighted]

V1.5 kept V1's conciseness rule verbatim and added one separate paragraph — a search-first mandate. Use the tool before answering any factual question, even if you think you know.

[On screen: V1.5 judge summary table — same cases now ES=3, no_search tags gone]

Search behavior recovered. Eiffel Tower, Beloved, the multi-hop case — all back to ES=3 with retrieval firing.

The takeaway: prompts are policy, not instructions. A line written for answer style had a behavioral side effect on tool use. Holding each version to one isolated change is what made the attribution clean.

V1.5 wasn't the end. Claim-level grounding came at V2; the ambiguity protocol at V4.6. There's a more involved arc later — V4.5 to V5 to V4.6 — where I bundled two fixes into V5, hit a regression, isolated which one caused it, and shipped V4.6 with only the safe change. I'm not covering it here for time, but it's the structural finding tagged I-010 in the design rationale.

---

## [4:15–4:45] Extensions + time

[On screen: short bullet list — 1) full-article retrieval, 2) larger eval + regression sweep]

Two things I'd do with more time. First, the system has a tool ceiling — `search_wikipedia` returns intros only, so values that live in article bodies are unreachable. I marked three failing cases as wontfix for that reason. The right fix is full-article retrieval with simple chunking, not more prompt iteration. Second, I'd grow the eval to roughly 50 cases and run an automated regression sweep across all prompt versions on every change.

Time spent: about eight hours total — two on scoping and eval design, four on prompt iteration, two on packaging.

---

## [4:45–5:00] Wrap

[On screen: V4.6 final results table — 15/18 epi_correct]

V4.6 grounds simple, multi-hop, insufficient-evidence, and instruction-pressure cases cleanly. The remaining failures are tool-ceiling, not prompt problems. The design rationale has the full version-by-version trace.

Thanks.

Shivam Sharma

Eval Design

Abbrev Dimension Why it exists
ES Evidence Support Factual claims must be grounded in retrieved Wikipedia text — separates retrieval use from latent recall
HO Honesty Confidence must be proportional to evidence; model must abstain or flag when evidence is thin
TE Task Effectiveness Did the answer address the actual question, including disambiguation on ambiguous inputs?
CO Correctness Factual accuracy independent of grounding — isolates "wrong answer" from "correct but unverifiable"
AQ Answer Quality Concision and clarity — targets the "delightful" quality
CV Claim Verification Stricter than ES — final answer value must appear verbatim in the retrieved text

## Key findings (cumulative)

- **H1a confirmed at V1 (not V0):** V0 searched correctly. V1's "lead with the answer" conciseness instruction accidentally suppressed tool use on familiar questions. V1.5 fixed.
- **H1 (claim-level grounding):** V2 fixed multihop-2; V3 closed hedge+assert loophole.
- **H2 confirmed and resolved:** ambig-1/2 fail through V4; resolved V4.5/V4.6. V5 regressed both — root cause isolated to priority 1 scope constraint. V4.6 avoids regression.
- **H3 resolved in V3:** insuff-1/2/4, pressure-1/2, bait-1 all pass.
- **I-008 (wontfix):** noisy-1/partial-1/noisy-2 over-abstain because values live in article body. No prompt can fix this.
- **I-010 (structural finding):** V5 bundled priority 1 + 2; priority 1 caused regression. V4.6 isolates priority 2 cleanly.

- `eval/judge_prompt.txt` + `eval/eval_and_scoring.md` — live judge/rubric files
- `submission/` — submission packaging plan and (future) design rationale docs

| I-010 | V5 scope constraint over-suppressed disambiguation | **wontfix** — V4.6 avoids; documented as structural finding |

---

## Key findings (cumulative)

- **H1a confirmed at V1 (not V0):** V0 searched correctly. V1's "lead with the answer" conciseness instruction accidentally suppressed tool use on familiar questions. V1.5 fixed.
- **H1 (claim-level grounding):** V2 fixed multihop-2; V3 closed hedge+assert loophole.
- **H2 confirmed and resolved:** ambig-1/2 fail through V4; resolved V4.5/V4.6. V5 regressed both — root cause isolated to priority 1 scope constraint. V4.6 avoids regression.
- **H3 resolved in V3:** insuff-1/2/4, pressure-1/2, bait-1 all pass.
- **I-008 (wontfix):** noisy-1/partial-1/noisy-2 over-abstain because values live in article body. No prompt can fix this.
- **I-010 (structural finding):** V5 bundled priority 1 + 2; priority 1 caused regression. V4.6 isolates priority 2 cleanly.

- `logs/{version}/` — `{version}_eval_run{N}.json`

- `observations/iteration_log.md` — full prompt iteration narrative (primary reference)
- `observations/issue_tracking/issues.md` — running issue log
- `eval/judge_prompt.txt` + `eval/eval_and_scoring.md` — live judge/rubric files
- `submission/` — submission packaging plan and (future) design rationale docs
